does: This survey systematically catalogs adversarial attacks on Large Language Models, examining vulnerabilities that persist even in safety-aligned models trained with instruction tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback. The work organizes existing research into distinct categories: textual-only attacks that manipulate input prompts, multi-modal attacks exploiting vision-language model interactions, and specialized attacks targeting distributed systems like federated learning and multi-agent architectures. By documenting prevalent jailbreak techniques demonstrated against production models like ChatGPT and Bard, the survey establishes a comprehensive taxonomy of attack vectors and their underlying exploitation mechanisms, grounding the emerging field at the intersection of NLP security and trustworthy machine learning.
connects: This work builds on foundational adversarial ML research that has long studied neural network robustness, extending classical adversarial perturbation concepts to the discrete, semantic domain of language. It responds directly to the rapid proliferation of capable LLMs and their integration into consequential applications, which has outpaced security evaluation frameworks. The survey synthesizes emerging jailbreak research—informal findings circulating in practitioner communities—into a rigorous academic treatment, connecting to prior work on instruction tuning robustness, RLHF failure modes, and prompt injection vulnerabilities. It positions itself within the broader trustworthy AI movement that examines alignment, interpretability, and safety of large models, acknowledging that theoretical alignment techniques may have practical limitations when confronted with adaptive adversaries.
enables: This comprehensive survey establishes a foundation for future defense mechanisms by clarifying the landscape of vulnerabilities and attack methodologies. It enables development of more robust alignment techniques by identifying systematic failure modes in current safety training approaches. The categorical framework supports designing specialized defenses for different attack surfaces—prompting strategies, multi-modal inputs, and distributed system architectures—and facilitates standardized evaluation benchmarks for adversarial robustness in LLMs. By articulating open research questions, the survey points toward detecting and mitigating attacks in deployment, developing certified defenses against adversarial inputs, and creating inherently more robust model architectures that withstand adversarial pressure rather than relying solely on post-hoc alignment training.
